FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, n=9,474 participants aged 9-10 at baseline, ABCD Data Release 5.1 (January 2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (self-reported hours on electronic devices: TV, texting, gaming, social media) and social media use (self-reported hours per week, measured separately). B1 applies: separate SM measurement with own coefficients reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) and multivariable linear regression models with coefficients, p-values, and 95% CIs reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients in main text and Tables 3-4: e.g., Group 4 anxiety β=1.44 (95% CI: 0.75, 2.12), depression β=2.94 (95% CI: 2.36, 3.63); Group 3 depression interaction β=9.29 (95% CI: 5.38, 13.21)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining longitudinal screen time/social media trajectories → anxiety/depression outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): Screen time/social media are independent variables predicting mental health outcomes (anxiety/depression), NOT dependent variables. Paper examines SM→non-SM-outcome relationship, not demographic→SM.
notes: Paper uses trajectory analysis to identify heterogeneous patterns of screen time and social media use. Examined moderation by substance use (alcohol/e-cigarette), but prevalence was very low (1.5%), limiting power. All inclusion criteria clearly met; EC7 does not apply as SM is exposure/predictor, not outcome.
